In communication, if there is an inability to see what is there, you cannot force someone to see it, just as you cannot force a drug addict to see the good life. Likewise, an inability to afford grace in communication, that the other person is trying to communicate something, something more than their own failure of comprehension, denies the inner life present in all of us, through the grace of God.

Hamann’s Faith

A confession must be made. The debates above reveal an archetype of sorts, of a person sealed inside a circle that has no outside, unable to receive what does not fit the predetermined form. What becomes clear in such moments is that argument alone cannot open what has been closed. Only a burgeoning from the inside may begin to crack the hardened shell of systems-thinking formalism. Something supplementary is needed, a therapy that targets the very aspiration to systemic totality which Hegel inherits from the German Idealist tradition and radicalizes. The person to offer such a therapy is the “Magus of the North”, Johann Georg Hamann.

I have spent years myself inside this circle of circles, I know it’s language, it’s satisfactions, I know it’s Euclidean centrality. And I know what it costs to leave, to slowly recognize that the circle, for all its solidarity, cannot contain what God provides. Relationality is felt, but grace arrives unbidden, a confession must be made:

There is no pure reason. Reason is never abstract, and never universal, nor self-standing. It always belongs to a particular planet, a particular zeitgeist, shaped by a particular language, and tradition. The Hegelian system, for example, for all its claims to absolute knowing, remains immensely humanist: it can only ever be a relationality that is intelligible to us, not to a relationality to the object itself. What sense would it make to speak of a relationality we have no relationship with? The very question lends us the answer.

We approach this limit constantly through analogy. The relationship between God and humanity, the divine and the human, is an analogical one, based on the free act of God in the Incarnation. The union of two natures in Christ is a mystery to be believed and adored, rather than something to be reconciled by speculative reason. The relationship is open because the kind of operative protocol the concept undergoes is incapable of getting from the human to the divine. There is a gap that can only be crossed by a gift from the other side. The attempt to expunge reason of its contingency is the proton pseudos of systematicity and the beginning of the neglect of faith. Reason’s purpose is to reveal its own limits. Like the law in Pauline theology, reason is a pedagogue. It leads us to the end of our own resources, exhausting its own resources until what is left is what is. Surely, Hegel’s methodology can be described as a series of self-exhaustions of the system itself. But for Hegel, this exhaustion leads to an ever higher integration, an apotheosis of reason. For Hamann, it leads elsewhere entirely. Reasons self-critique is meant to show us our ignorance and need for something beyond ourselves. When reason claims to be sufficient, it becomes idolatrous. Reason itself is a fallen creature, a fallen human capacity. It is given sensations, given a tradition, given a language, and ultimately given revelation. Its job is to acknowledge this dependence, not to spin everything out of itself. Thus, reason’s ultimate purpose is to lead us to the end of our own resources. It is meant to fail, to show us the abyss. This is the exact opposite of Hegel’s project, where reason’s self-examination leads to its own apotheosis. For Hamann, reason’s examination leads only to its own limit. What lies beyond that limit is not for reason to say.

Perhaps what lies beyond that limit, what is given is language. In the beginning was the Word, after all. Hamann has the view that language is prior to reason. To an Idealist, this would seem like a crude, empirical assertion which the concept would sublate (yes I will use that word). They would almost immediately assert: “Prior? prior in what sense? Chronologically? Empirically? This would not make sense, given it would be anthropological, rather than philosophical. Spirit sublates such empirical conditions into its own self-conscious logical development. The truth of language is the Concept!”.

Hamann is certainly not making a claim about cavemen grunting before being able to think. He is making a humbler claim about the nature of all thought- that language is the inescapable medium of all thought. You cannot get behind it or beyond it to a purer realm of thought, whether that realm is Kant’s transcendental unity of apperception, or Hegel’s pure self-thinking thought. The idea of a thought that has fully sublated its linguistic expression into a pure, self-present meaning is an illusion. Steiner himself has commented on how a spiritual person like Hamann would be quick to jump against the position that meaning is not immanent, or located within some “outside”. He would likely say to Hegel’s Concept, if one were to hold the position that the form is “sublated” (and in some sense contingent to the word, whether it be the rational natural contingency of form, or the formalism of the Understanding which only values symbolic coherence) that for all its claimed concreteness it is just another attempt to vault over the abyss of language’s being, which is itself being.

Hamann often uses the term “hysteron proteron” to accuse both Kant, and by extension, any idealist system of getting things backwards. Such a position would treat the a priori (the logical, conceptual) as if it were the foundation, when in fact it is derived from and always dependent upon the a posteriori. The genealogical priority of language means that what philosophy claims is first (the Concept) is actually “last”. Or as scripture puts it: so the last shall be first, and the first last. The very possibility of an abstract logical determination purifying itself of its linguistic and embodied skin is denied by Hamann. There is no thought before or outside language. There is only thought in language, shaped by it and carried by it. Consider a practical example. What is gender? One approach might attempt a rational determination: gender is logically rooted in the sexed distinction, a clean conceptual definition which admits no variability (although it certainly leaves room for it). This approach, for all its claim to necessity, tells you nothing about what gender actually is! It is indistinguishable from a dogmatic traditionalism that simply asserts the union of gender and sex. The alternative is to attend to how gender appears through particular cultures, how it is lived, and spoken, and named. This second approach does not begin with the concept and apply it to the world. It begins with the world, with language and tradition, and lets the concept emerge from there. The concept, in this case, is not the foundation, but the result. It is last, not first. This is essentially Hamann’s point. Language is not conceived of as the clothing of the thought, it is its body. You cannot strip it away to find the pure concept underneath, because there is nothing underneath. The concept is not behind the word, it is in the word, inseparable from it, given with it. And this is why reason, which thought itself sovereign, must confess its dependence. Reason does not invent language for itself, it is born into language, it relates the word to itself. It does not master language, it is mastered by it. Reason is pedagogue to Christ. The ultimate purpose of reason and its self-critique is to lead us to the end of our own resources. It is meant to fail, to show us the “abyss,” so that we might stop trusting in it and instead trust in the Word that is given to us. This is the exact opposite of Hegel’s project, where reason’s self-examination leads to its own apotheosis, what Steiner calls logical fetishism (for more reasons than this).

There are some relations you have to take on faith, relations which reason itself cannot immediately show you. For example, it is inconceivable to see karmic links between people within a framework like Kant’s or Hegel’s. The Incarnation is another example. In Christ, divine and human natures unite without fusion, without one side being reduced to the other. You cannot arrive at the self-relationality of Christ by reasoning, because this system of relationality is not exhaustive of Christ’s self-relationality, it is only exhaustive of the intelligibility reason has. This is why faith is not a substitute for reason, nor a moment to be subsumed within reason’s development. Faith is the mode of relating to what exceeds us- to the God who gives himself, to the Word that speaks to us, and to the relations that hold us together, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit. Faith is the trust that the gift is good, and that the giver is faithful. In the future this mode of relation will develop itself into a new faculty. Nevertheless, this will be for a future time, brought along by a revolution as paradigm shifting as non-Euclidean geometry was for the Kantians.

item.title: Idle Talk in the Age of TikTok: On Linguistic Decay
item.description:
Of all the human faculties, language is perhaps the most intimate and mysterious, being responsible for conjuring emotions in the soul, and drawing out latent potential, all by an invocation of symbols and signs that we use daily. In its original and true nature, language was Logos, the divine creative principle that ordered the cosmos. The Word was understood in the past as an active power, a vibration that participated directly in the essence of what it named. There existed no arbitrary divide between the signifier and the signified, sound and being been fused. To utter a true name was therefore not to label a thing from the outside, but to invoke its reality and align with its spiritual core. This ontological unity rendered language inherently sacramental. Blessings, curses, and ritual incantations were seen as having immediate metaphysical effects because speech was an act of cosmic participation, distinct from its conception today as mere formality or social convention. This sanctity gave rise to the concept of holy languages. Traditions revered Sanskrit, Hebrew, Examination Chinese, Quranic Arabic, Biblical Latin, and so on and so forth, as Marshall McLuhan rightfully commented, as revealed architectures, or novel vessels for truth. To think, pray, or compose in these languages was to operate within a (and the) divine framework, the only medium capable of housing certain transcendental realities. Furthermore, this unity was experienced sensibly. The resonance of a word was organically tied to its meaning. Phenomena like the Bouba/Kiki effect (where people intuitively associate rounded sounds with rounded shapes and vice versa) are thus interpreted as the surviving remnants of this primordial unity. They are fleeting proof that the human soul retains a memory of a time when form and essence, or sound and sense, were one.

An ancient view this is, if for the fact that it casts our contemporary experience of language into a puzzling relief. For, we do not live in a world of true names and efficacious words. We inhabit a universe of arbitrary signs, where cat could just as easily be “gato”, “neko”, or “Katze”, as a matter of social agreement and contingency (or in some cases a view which deprives language of spiritual significance but has to concede that it fits mechanically in one way or another) rather than an explicit connection with cosmic truth. There is not a discrepancy, this in fact evidences a great decay from language as a participatory event, into convention. The fall from logos is the story of language becoming unmoored from being, a process intrinsically linked to the evolution of consciousness itself. This shift is a necessary, if not arduous, stage in Spirit’s journey. It corresponds to the epochal awakening of the Consciousness Soul - the modern principle of self-reflective individualism. To achieve self-awareness and freedom, human consciousness had to turn decisively inward, withdrawing its projective participation from the world. This inward turn, mirroring modernity’s deterritorializing force, required the very severing of the ancient bond between word and thing. Language had to be drained of its innate albeit given meaning, so that the sovereign “I” could become capable of grasping meaning from within as well as without. The celebrated arbitrariness of the sign, therefore, is in a way a diagnostic mark of this age. What linguistics posits as a neutral principle is the very mechanism of alienation required for the birth of the modern ego. The word becomes an empty shell, a detached signifier, because the conscious self needed a neutral tool to wield, rather than a sacred power to which it was subject.

The historical indices of this fall are certainly tangible. The evolution from pictographic to phonetic writing is a tell-tale sign. Hieroglyphs and ideograms maintained an iconic, mimetic link to the world. For example, a picture of a sun still carried a trace of the sun’s essence. The alphabet, a triumph of abstraction, reduces speech to a code of neutral symbols representing pure sound. This form of language is the perfect technology (in the sense of techne) for the Consciousness Soul, seeing as it is analytic efficient, and severed from iconic presence. In addition to this, it enables the dissemination of abstract thought, but at the same time finalizes the divorce of the written word from the living cosmos. This abstraction was compounded by the “scientific” and nominalist worldviews, which exiled qualities and essences from the material world, leaving only quantities and names. Language, in this world, can only point to surfaces, and likewise the commonality which etymology sheds light on can only reasonably signify a correlation of concept with the convenience of spatiotemporal form. Furthermore, the Bouba/Kiki effect now appears in a tragic light as a testament to what was lost. It tells of old modes of consciousness and participatory magic (and language, back then, was by all means magic) flaring up in a mind otherwise conditioned to see sound and shape as separate domains. It is the soul’s muted protest against its own conceptual imprisonment.

Thus, the modern condition of language as a formal system, as a convenient but hollow geometry for the ego’s use, is the subjective reality of this objective historical stage. The feeling that language is an internal combinatory space is not its eternal truth, it is only what is left after the spirit of Logos has withdrawn. However, it is not all dire. The great emptying of meaning in the world, can also be considered a great preparation. It is the necessary negative moment in the process of totality. By passing through this desert of abstraction, by experiencing language as a detached and formal medium, the Consciousness Soul gains the unprecedented capacity for critical self-reflection and free conceptual recognition. It must lose the given, participatory Word, to eventually find it again, consciously and from within. The arbitrary language of the modern age is therefore the condition in which the possibility of a future redeemed speech is created. Having fully integrated the power of abstract thought, humanity will one day speak with the authority of Logos.

For now, we have to deal with what Heidegger called “Idle Talk”. The ultimate manifestation of this emptied, circulating language, is what Heidegger characterizes as speech that has completely lost its anchor in the primordial, disclosive power of Logos. It is language reduced to a self-referential social mechanism. Words are passed along not to reveal the truth of a matter, but precisely to avoid encountering it. In Idle Talk, understanding/substance (they are etymologically the same word, fun fact!) is simulated through the effortless absorption of pre-digested and “average” meanings. The reality of the arbitrary sign is a closed circuit of chatter where words refer only to other words, creating a comforting shallow consensus that shields the modern ego from the demanding world-disclosing responsibility of authentic speech. This is the sacred Word that once invoked being, having devolved, in its most common social form, into a background noise of cliché and hearsay- it is the sound of consciousness speaking to itself from within itself.

A descent into abstraction needs a habitat, and it finds this habitat in the digital realm of the internet, the perfect expression of modernity’s de-temporalized, deterritorialized space. Here, the inward-turning Consciousness Soul constructs a world in its own image featuring a realm of pure information where language exists in a state of frictionless circulation, liberated not only from the object, but from the being and body of the Earth. The internet is the ecosystem in which idle talk becomes the dominant, self-replicating mode of being, setting the state for a platform like TikTok to emerge as the apotheosis of post-literate communication.

The linguistic ecology of TikTok represents the logical result of this trajectory in the form of the metastasis of Idle Talk into an optimized and fully realized system. The platform’s hallmarks of gratuitous abbreviation, intentional misspellings, syntactic compression, and the rapid, vampiric repurposing of words (particularly from African American Vernacular English) into mimetic signals are the signature of language accelerating into pure social functionality, shedding any remaining pretensions to depth, history, or disclosure. When “because” becomes “bc”, or “this shit” becomes “ts”, or complex (in form, not in substance) statements are cramped into acronyms, we witness the final abstraction of the sign. The word lacks being even a full shell- it is fragment. It is a token whose value is determined solely by its velocity and adhesive power within the circuit of communal recognition.

This is Idle Talk weaponized and accelerated by the habitat of the internet. Heidegger’s concept feared a chatter that levels all understanding to the average, whereas TikTok’s architecture actively enforces it. Algorithmic virality rewards surface level novelty and in-group fluency, incentivizing the continuous production of linguistic shortcuts that bypass conscious reflection by the subject, in order to trigger instant affective or tribal response. The intentional “wrongness” of a spelling is a performative rejection of external authority, creating a closed vernacular that fortifies community boundaries on the one hand, and further divorces the sign from any stable referent on the other. “Rizz” and “sigma” are not in a dictionary (yet, as far as I know), but still exists in the collective performance of its use as a purely circulating social value.

In a sense, we can call this beyond illiteracy (although America has a major illiteracy problem, and you’d expect nothing less from a democratic nation), into post-literacy, defined as the linguistic state perfectly adapted to the Consciousness Soul’s internalization. Language has become a pure tool of the sovereign ego, constructing transient identities and affiliations in a digital das Man. However, even within this schema, even in the apparent decay, a purpose is served. TikTok language, as the extreme of Idle Talk, makes the alienation of the sign total and unmistakable. By reducing communication to its most fluid and disposable form, it forces a kind of crisis. One either surrenders to endless circulation for its own sake, or begins to feel the hunger for a Word that means. In this sense, the vernacular of the platform serves as a negative positivity. It is the resonant sound of the hollow Logos, waiting to be filed again by a spirit that has fully passed through the task of its own grasping, waiting to be recognized from within and from without.

item.title: Libya under Gaddafi: The Only African Country in North Africa?
item.description:
To be or not to be (African)?

The recent AFCON 2025 win by Senegal (versus Morocco) has brought to mind an inflammatory question, or rather an observation, which casts doubt on the nature of “North Africa” in the context of being a part of Africa. North Africa’s identity is the site of a fundamental antagonism wherein objectively it is an inescapable part of the African continent by every material measure (such as geologically it is located on the continent, and it’s history, which includes its political economy and historical trade routes and such). At the same time subjectively, its people’s self-determined identity is overwhelmingly anchored in the MENA (Middle East and North Africa) civilizational sphere, often defined in explicit contrast to “Africa”. This contradiction doesn’t strike me as a mistake to be solved, but the central dogma of the region’s modern condition. The very passion by which it disidentifies itself is proof of an unsettled relationship, rather than lack thereof. I am speaking, of course, of the anti-Blackness present in North Africa.

Ask a Moroccan or an Algerian if they are “African”, and see the varied response one will be met with. The answer, when and if it comes, is rarely a simple yes, barre the cases when conceding the very attachment of geography isn’t the framing of the question (so, answers like “of course it’s African, it is in Africa!”). “African”, if used at all, is often appended as a geographical afterthought- “we are in Africa, but we are not of it”. This is the crux of what can be called a painful schism. On the one side lies the objective and unchangeable fact of the being. On the other, lies the subjective force of self-conception which aligns itself historically, culturally, and religiously, with the Middle East and the Mediterranean; an alignment with the Arab-Islamic world of the MENA region. In a way, North Africa inherits Europe’s defining mode of metaphysics and interface with the cosmos as “a forgetting of Being”, while at the same time being rooted differently, or at least partially conditioned by, Africa. Clearly, this goes beyond syntactics and into semantics. To dismiss it with the pussyfooted stance some may call diplomatic, that North Africa is simply “both” is a cheap cop-out. It throws dirt on the actual self-identity of millions for whom “African” is not a badge of belonging, but an indication of a history where distance was constructed through centuries of civilization and trade.

Before we can ask if North Africa is a part of Africa, we must first as: What do we even mean by Africa? The continent’s very name is an external imposition. To the ancient Greeks and Romans, the land south of the Mediterranean was not “Africa” but Libya, a coastal strip distinct from the interior. The term Africa likely stems from the Roman designation “Africa Proconsularis” for their province around Carthage, and was gradually expanded by European cartographers and colonizers. By the 19th century Scramble for Africa, it solidified as the label for the continent. In this sense, it’s very self-conception is wrought with externality. However, the case can be made that the imposed frame was reclaimed from within via the anti-colonial and Pan-African movements of the 20th century, led by Ghana’s Kwame Nkrumah, and Tanzania’s Julius Nyerere as a rallying cry for solidarity and shared destiny. This was further institutionalized through the founding of the Organization of African Unity. Here, “Africa” has become an idea of unity against oppression (but still externally determined). Despite this, in popular vernacular a kind of reductionism persists. Globally, and importantly in some cases within North Africa itself, the term “African” has been racially narrowed to a metonym for sub-Saharan Africa. It carries a connotation of Blackness, and a perceived cultural sphere separate from the Mediterranean and Arab worlds. This reduction is a trap which instantly ensnares our initial question, making “Are you African?” feel, to many North Africans, like being asked “Are you Black, or are you from that other Africa?”. The question of “Africa’s” determination has yet to be proven, and has only been theorized as of thus far. The true test of it as a distinct being will only be proven in the actualized existence of its people. The more focused question one can ask then is “what is North Africa, such that it brings about distinction from Africa ‘proper’?”. To answer this question, it is necessary to point out a fundamental divide within the Arab-Islamic world itself: the Maghreb (الْمَغْرِب‎ - “the West,” encompassing Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Libya, and often Mauritania) versus the Mashriq (الْمَشْرِق‎ - “the East,” the Arab states of the Middle East). A North African’s primary civilizational orientation is often first to this Maghreb-Mashriq continuum, not to a pan-African one.

Beneath this level lies the substance of the Amazigh, or Berber, people. These are the indigenous peoples of North Africa, with languages and cultures stretching back millennia before Phoenician or Arab arrival, and are the uncontested “African” layer of the region. To acknowledge the Amazigh is to acknowledge that North Africa’s deepest roots are autonomously and uniquely African, and not imported from the Middle East. This complicates any simple Arab vs Africa binary, and is key to understanding the regions’ character. Additionally, geography dictates an exception. The Nile Valley (primarily in Egypt and Sudan) forms a distinct corridor. Egypt’s history and psyche are fundamentally shaped by the river flowing from the African heartland. Sudan, even more explicitly, is a historical pivot point, a vast country torn by conflicts often framed as Arab versus African. This corridor ties North-Eastern Africa inextricably to the Sahel and the Horn, blurring any neat boundary and reminding us that the Sahara’s edges are zones of gradation and not distinct hard lined walls. Thus, we begin with contested definitions with “Africa” as an imposed label, a reclaimed project, and a racially narrowed colloquialism. “North Africa” is a modern geopolitical term masking a mixed reality of Arab-Islamic identity, Amazigh indigeneity, and the unifying exception of the Nile. It is within this tangled semantic field that the larger conflict plays out.

However fiercely identity may pull toward the Mediterranean and the Mashriq, North Africa remains wedded to the continent by chains of geography, history, and modern necessity. This is the objective, inarguable substrate upon which all subjective identities in the region have to contend with and integrate (sometimes unsuccessfully). North Africa rests upon the African tectonic plate; its mountains, faults, and mineral wealth are born of the same continental processes as the rest of Africa. The Sahara, so often imagined as a “barrier” is in fact a shared system which stretches from the Atlas foothills to the Sahelian shore. It is a connector that demands specialized adaptation, fostering trans-Saharan networks. Most definitively, the Nile River ( which we may call Egypt’s raison d’être) is an African river. Its lifeblood flows from the Ethiopian Highlands and the Great Lakes region. Egypt’s destiny is therefore irrevocably tied to the management, politics, and climate of the African interior. The historical tether cannot be ignored either, long predating the era of the Arab-Islamic. Ancient Egypt’s civilization cannot be understood outside its perpetual dialogue (whether through trade, warfare, and migration) with Nubia (aka modern Sudan), and cultures further South. The Kingdom of Kush conquered and ruled Egypt, a profound African reversal of power. Deeper in the desert, civilizations like the Garamantes (in modern Libya) built sophisticated subterranean irrigation systems and acted as crucial intermediaries, proving the Sahara was a space of commerce. Foundational to all is the Amazigh, the indigenous inhabitants whose presence for thousands of years constitutes the incontestable truth that the first “North Africans” were, and are, an African people.

The final link to “Africa proper” is the political-economic necessity of the 21st century. The various crises of the Sahel, where ever they come from (looking at you France), do not respect colonial borders, and spill northward. Meaning the instability in Mali or Niger becomes a direct security threat to Algeria, Tunisia, and Libya. Membership in the African Union also acts as a strategic platform for conflict mediation and collective bargaining. To abandon it would be to forfeit influence and abdicate responsibility for the systems which directly impact North African security and prosperity. North Africa is, in effect, bound by the objective fact sovereignty, which ties it to Africa. Thus, while a North African might look eastward toward Mecca in prayer, they stand atop African history and the African future of shared crises and fortunes. Any complete disavowal of “Africa” is a flight from the world. The land one stands on insists upon the very connection to Africa which some North Africans intend to disavow.

It’s strange, then, that the anchor is so very clearly African, but identity decisively points east. The allegiance of the region is certainly not to a continental project, it is towards the civilizational sphere of the MENA world as an active daily substance. It becomes less strange when one familiarizes themselves with a few “pillars” if you will. The first is the religion. Islam is the primary communal bond, and the foundational layer of social and spiritual life. Its culture and moral framework orient the believer towards the holy cities of Mecca and Medina in the Arabian Peninsula, and not towards the African interior. The Ummah is the imagined polity whose heartlands lie in Asia. The language goes hand in hand with Islam. Arabic is the language of the sacred text of the Quran, media, and politics. The lingua franca that ties a Moroccan student to a Lebanese novel, or an Algerian politician to a Qatari news (Al Jazeera comes to mind) is Islam. This shared linguistic worlds creates a continuity with the Mashriq, making Cairo and Beirut feel culturally closer than Bamako or Abuja. The daily cultural diet does not dissuade the continuity, either. The dominance of Arab satellite television (like the aforementioned Al Jazeera, MBC, etc.), and the circulation of Arab music, or Ramadan television dramas, creates a social world through the digital which faces East. One final factor to take into account is the historical context of Ottoman rule or influence, which brought centuries of integration into an Eastern empire, and the later anti-colonial struggle framed through the potent ideology of Pan-Arabism. Gamal Abdel Nasser or Ba’athist parties are dreams of Arab unity, not of African unity. The political imagination stands as horizontally Arab, not African.

Not African and not Arab? Not realistic.

This consolidation of MENA identity conditions an active dis-identification. “African”, often reductively coded as Black and sub-Saharan becomes the distinguishing other by which the social process that separates “us” from “them” takes place. This is heard in colloquial or casual speech and seen in social attitudes toward darker-skinned Africans. This attitude is reflected in media representations that subtly or overtly frame “Arab” North Africa as more civilized, developed, or historically significant than “Black” Africa. The distancing taking place here is hierarchical, and acts as a mechanism which maintains the coherence of the preferred self-conception as being solely in continuity with the Middle East, and coincidentally being placed in Africa. Thus, when asked about identity, the heart’s answer is drawn from this shared reality of politics, religion, culture, language, media, and so on and so forth, all flowing from the East. The African ground, for all its material weight, remains for many, a silent geographical contingency, overshadowed by the life of Arab-Islamic civilization. This is the powerful subjective truth that any honest analysis must confront.

With respect to this, we have a contradiction at the crux of North Africa’s identity. The vehement distancing from “Africa” is not evidence by a lack of relationship, it is the loudest pronouncement of its unresolved relationship with it. One does not build an identity against something that is irrelevant. The force of the rejection mirrors the depth of the historical “entanglement”, which is putting it lightly. The primary source of this ordeal is found in the Trans-Saharan Slave Trade. For over a thousand years, this civilizational-scale process crafted a brutal social world where the “African”, particularly the Black African from south of the desert, became synonymous with “other” and “servile” in North African societies. This process embedded a structural and psychological anti-Blackness into the fabric of the social relations, establishing a hierarchy of human value that placed the Arabized or Amazigh North African above the “abeed” (slave, a term still used derogatorily). This is the origin of the separation, a hierarchy native to the nation, but certainly reinforced by European colonialism. French and Italian administrators, armed with the pseudoscience of racial anthropology, pursued explicit “Mediterraneanist” or “Berberist” policies. They argued that North Africans (particularly Amazigh populations) were a white and noble race, distinct from and superior to the Negroes of sub-Saharan Africa. This attitude, being internalized by segments of the local elite, added a modern European-sanctioned racial taxonomy to the older Arabo-Islamic one. Being offered a bargain which North Africans could not refuse, they were to be placed on a higher rung of the colonial racial ladder, contingent on accepting their own differentiation from the content below. It is not a coincidence that the term here is bargain, harkening back to the Faustian Bargain, and the Heideggerian ‘forgetting of being” mentioned that North Africa has inherited from Europe. This is just one avenue to be explored.

In the modernist project of building independent nation-states, anti-Blackness became a functional “moment of differentiation”. For North African elites forging post-colonial identities, they required a “constitutive outside”, or an “other”, against which the modern national self could be defined. The constructed “Black African” serves this purpose perfectly. Defining the modern “Arab-Maghrebi” citizen involved a conscious (and in some cases unconscious) differentiation from this other, purifying the national-self image and aligning it with a perceived lineage of historic (Phoenician, Roman, Arab) Mediterranean civilization, in contrast to a stereotyped “primitive” and “tribal” Africa. We have now a contemporary performance of that centuries-old differentiation, a socio-political ritual that reinforces the borders of a non-African national identity. The scorn for the Black migrant is, tragically and ironically, a perverse reaffirmation of a separate self. North Africa’s “non-African” identity is, to a significant degree, built upon the history of its relationship with and by virtue of Africa.

To view this struggle as between Arab and African, is to miss the signals originating from North Africa itself which problematize this binary. We can look to the Amazigh Revival as an indigenous corrective. The fight for Tamazight language and culture is a reclamation of an African identity which predates Arabization by a thousand years. In this assertion, what is to be found is an assertion of North African identity that is uniquely African, yet distinct from Arab political hegemony and sub-Saharan cultures. Further south is the Sahara-Sahel zone, where one can interpret this land as a land of synthesis and differentiation- one which purist ideologies will be forced to categorize incorrectly, or count as a deviation from the norm of a worldview. Nations like Mali, Niger, and Sudan are crucibles where Arab and Amazigh North Africa has blended with Songhai, Fulani, and Nubian cultures for millennium. Timbuktu’s manuscripts, are written in Arabic script but preserve the knowledge of the Malian empire. This is a good example of “fusion”. These societies render the harsh distinction between northern littoral and southern forests moot.

Gaddafi

Gaddafi’s Libya stands apart. Isolated in the Arab world, he declared himself “an African, catalyzing a decade of objective institutional creativity aimed at nothing less than the political and economic unification of the African continent. His government became the primary architect and financier of the African Union’s transformative leap from the loose OAU to a more integrated body. Libya funded over 15% of the AU’s budget, championed the creation of the African Investment Bank and the African Monetary Fund, and was the driving force behind RASCOM, the first satellite for African telecommunications, breaking a foreign monopoly. He leveraged state wealth to build infrastructure across the continent and positioned Libya as the patron for liberation movements and marginalized regions. Gaddafi represented a tangible, institutional answer to the question of continental unity that far exceeded the symbolic gestures of other North African states. Unfortunately, this objective reality being created existed in a stark parallel to a subjective social reality it failed to fundamentally alter, or rather, did not get to alter. While the state built African unity, Libyan society, which carried the historical baggage of the trans-Saharan slave trade and hierarchical social structures, continued to exhibit widespread anti-Black prejudice This was visible in the treatment of sub-Saharan migrants. Gaddafi’s Pan-Africanism did not constitute a national reckoning with racism or stem from a grassroots embrace of African identity. This paradox (which is in reality, the paradox of North African identity) reached its climax after the 2011 NATO-led intervention. The instant evaporation of the Pan-African project revealed its precarious foundation- that it was an edifice built on state power and not on broad objective spiritual conviction. The subsequent descent of Libya into chaos including the resurgence of open slave markets targeting Black Africans, and the collapse of living standards (from one of Africa’s highest literacy rates and GDP per capita to a failed state) demonstrated that Gaddafi’s noble project of unification was doomed to fail if it did not reconstitute North African identity, or reconcile what it actually means to be a North African. The very fact that slavery re-emerged tells you something intrinsically about the social substance of the Libyan people, and by extension North Africans as a whole. That the post-Gaddafi landscape did not revert to a neutral “Libyan” identity, instead regressing into the raw, unmediated societal pathologies and tribal factions that the state had previously contained and channeled, illustrates that this was a reversion to an older, unhealed spirit. In other words, the reappearance of slavery is the return of the repressed historical spirit, proof that the social substance of the people had not been transformed. Most, if not all countries (barre Congo and Sudan), have confronted what it means to be an African (particularly this or that kind of African- Angolan, South African, Ethiopian, Kenyan, etc.), and so this brings a sense of closure to sub-Saharan countries that is felt in contrast when dealing with the North African countries. This is part of why Africans south of the Sahara may find it easier to forget North Africa, because they have not dealt with this intrinsic anti-Blackness. After all, besides Gaddafi’s attempt and the practical establishment of the OAN (of which Morocco left in 1984 as a protest for the OAN recognizing the SADR. It must also be stated that Western Sahara was not part of historic Morocco in any meaningful sovereign sense, and so leaving the OAN in 1984, then applying for membership in the EEC is not a good look), the only interface with North Africans that sub-Saharans have is from North Africans arguing why they should be able to say the n-word. There is no real nationally recognized effort in reconstituting any kind of North African identity.

Gaddafi’s project, therefore, was not wrong in its vision, but premature in its historical moment. It demonstrated that objective institutions cannot survive if they outpace the zeitgeist. One can say this was a failure of synchronization. The projects’ destruction demonstrates for us that the central unresolved problem for North Africa is existential. What does it mean to be North African? Until this question is answered in a way that reconciles body and spirit, heaven and earth, all grand political architectures in North Africa will remain vulnerable. The future of North Africa as an integral part of Africa does not hinge on more trade agreements or AU resolutions, though these are necessary. It hinges on an existential, spiritual reckoning that has yet to occur, one that is to be found in a collective and conscious confrontation with the historical that created its dis-identity. Until it does, North Africa will remain what it is today: the continent’s estranged shore, physically present but spiritually separated, building its identity upon the forgetting of its deepest and most intimate connection. The path belonging to North Africa is not eastward, it is inward, into a history it must remember down into the plains and forests across the desert, and inward into itself into a self it has yet to become.

item.title: Notes on Sexuality: Male and Female in Light of Spiritual Science
item.description:
Intro

The featured header image above depicts the figures of Tristan and Isolde. Their story is one that, on the surface, appears to be a medieval tale of romance and forbidden love. Tristan, a brave Cornish knight, is sent to Ireland to escort Isolde, an Irish princess, to marry his uncle, King Mark. During the journey, they accidentally drink a strong potion meant for Isolde and mark on their wedding night. The potion turns out to be a love potion, a potion which induces, or suggests, strong feelings of love toward another. As a result, it binds them in an irresistible passion. Despite marrying King Mark, Isolde and Tristan begin a secret affair, fraught with danger and suspenseful situations. Eventually they are discovered and forced to flee. The story ends in tragedy, with one version finding a wounded Tristan sending for Isolde, the only one who can hear him. He arranges for the ship carrying her to fly a white sail if she is aboard. His jealous wife lies, telling him the sail is back. Believing Isolde isn’t coming, Tristan dies of despair, Isolde arrives, finds him dead, and dies of grief beside him. In one sense, the story does tell of a forbidden love, in the sense that the true meaning behind the veil of this story is esoteric and not immediately accessible. To enter into this narrative is to observe an allure, drawing one into the familiar territory of the living forces within the human soul. Within Tristan one finds the consummate achiever, a master of sword and strategy. His world comprises action, conquest, and honor. However, beneath the armor resides a loneliness which no comrade’s praise can fill. When encountering Isolde, it is less a meeting of two people, and much more of a “completion”. He finds within her the embodiment of fulfillment. Likewise, his desire for her is a desperate, spiritual thirst mistaken for a worldly one. He is the masculine spirit, proficient in extending dominion over the shape of the external world, being encountered by a wave of unfamiliarity in feeling and soul. It would be correct to say that his tragedy is the belief that wholeness lies in another, turning love away from communion into fatalistic ends. Contrast this with Isolde, who begins as a figure defined by her function. She is the “other” to which Tristan remedies himself with. Archetypically, she can be considered a healer. And so, when ingesting the potion, both figures find themselves awakening to a different law in their hearts. In Tristan, Isolde finds the incarnation of the romantic, the opposite of the worldly ordered world of obligation (particularly since princess marriages were for political aims and not borne of true love). Her journey descends into the sovereignty of the heart, where all bonds and identities dissolve into a unitary connection. This is the mirror of the feminine dilemma; women find themselves resisting the urge to completely surrender themselves to Eros, where love becomes the totality of existence, in effect erasing the self it initially wanted to fulfill. Contained within these two figures is an immense magnetism, where each polarity contains one half of totality, and at once what the other needs. Yin-yang, being-non-being, day-night, sun-moon, and so on and so forth. Man exists as citizens of both regions, oscillating between each. To contemplate upon this fact of reality is to ask oneself, “what, essentially, are man and woman?”. Is their polarity merely some biological necessity, or social determination? Or does it point towards something more fundamental in the structure of human being? It is here we may begin an exploration with a distinct and, some might argue, provocative lens. Behind the veil or romance, this story points towards the dynamics foundational to man’s existence. Because the human spirit evolves through direct being and participation, existence itself is structured by the polarities of activity and receptivity, or any of the various polarities which define respective modes of being such as thought and feeling, or outward formation and inward space. To become “whole”, consciousness must fully understand (and inhabit, however this mechanism of reincarnation and spiritual economy is beyond the scope of this article) it’s opposite. The tension and attraction between what we call masculine and feminine principles is the primary schoolhouse for this education, and as such, Tristan and Isolde represent unconscious enactments of this law. Much time can be spent on any number of interpretations, dissecting this or that aspect of the story, etc., but our principal aim in this article is “male and female”. Correspondingly, we can define “male and female” or “man and woman” as the complementary modes through which a genderless human consciousness experiences earthly life in this period of time. Let this not be confused with static identities, for they are temporary orientations which themselves have been flipped and turned inside out at times.

Contemporary problems hold back our understandings of “man and woman”. Modern discourse on gender, for all its analytical rigor, operates within a framework of lacking. When it comes to sex, it can describe differences, whether through the lens of biological essentialism, or social constructivism, but it cannot ultimately answer why these differences should matter in any existential sense. At best, a conception can be developed which relies on the natural immediacy of the sexed distinction, out of which social institutions make use of this content and fashion what they will from it- typically the family structure which interfaces with civil society and the market. However, this lacks a freely determined telos for sex, let alone gender, which we can consider briefly as the configuration of polarities in the makeup of man (and of which we will deal with later). What all modern views lack is a coherent and unifying telos.

First, modern conceptions lack a meaningful account of sexual difference itself. They can catalogue distinctions as either dictated by genes or culture, but what of the archetypal magnetism that arises from these distinctions? The lack of explanation for that animating force allows many “New Age” beliefs, or any belief which is subjective and lacks rigor, to fill in the blanks. Without a purpose, a reason why these dynamics exist, difference is fated to be reduced to either a mechanical fact (of which mechanistic and chemist modes of being are to be used by something higher. This includes the solipsistic mechanism of the Concept, Hegelians aren’t excluded!) or arbitrary construction, rendering the draw between masculine and feminine principles as a byproduct of biological reproduction, or social fiction. A second lack is a sustainable model for the integration of the body and consciousness. The materialist view, for example, makes the body a cage of determinism, worshiping its god “chance”. Constructivism likewise treats the problem of consciousness as incidental. Neither satisfactorily explains how our desires, drives, or identities interact with our embodied reality. We are left with a gap between what we are told we are as an organism, or merely as a social subject, and what we experience ourselves to be as conscious beings inhabiting a gendered form with personal (and spiritual) urgency. A third lack can be the lack of a framework which reconciles universal patterns and infinite personal variation. What destiny, to be caught between the false choice of rigid categories, and relativity so total that it obliterates the very possibility of a common ground which it is generated by? These failures result in an intelligibility. We are unable to speak meaningfully about a common human experience shaped by these two polarities, while also honoring the novelty, or unique journey, of every individual.

Ultimately, and most importantly (for the philosophically minded), modern conceptions lack a telos. The most significant absence in our current understandings is what I initially mentioned- telos. By denying any inherent purpose or end goal to the masculine-feminine polarity, human relationships and gender identity are severed from any higher, ideal, cosmic, spiritual development. This can help describe the crisis of meaning we face today, or rather, this attitude is a byproduct of modernity. I surely don’t mean to imply that “solving gender roles” (read: resorting to dogmatic and blind traditionalism) will help us resolve the “crisis of meaning” in modern day. What I wish to underscore is that if this fundamental aspect of human being has no purpose beyond utility or subjective expression, then agonies and pleasures, such as those expressed in the tale of Tristan and Isolde, no matter how beautiful or sublime, are coincidences. Our longing for completion is dead on arrival, being rendered biochemical or social accidentals.

So, we will solve this problem. Not comprehensively, I can only provide suggestions and clarifications with the help of anthroposophy. For it to be a comprehensive solution, there is the implication that the reader takes it upon themselves to live the upcoming wisdom, in one form or another. I will post a “too long; didn’t read” section for those that wish to find a log line to orient themselves around:

TL;DR

The claim that there is “no difference” between men and women is misleading, it relies on a higher meaning that simply isn’t there. It is true that the “I”, or ego, of a person is genderless, but to claim this in the face of immediate biological differentiation is invoking an esoteric knowledge that the masses imply don’t intend to convey. On the other hand, insisting that these differences are representative of a difference in kind, rather than mode, is even stupider. Men and women are much more alike than they are different. We are capable of the same intellectual and creative feats, barre certain physiological predispositions. Where they often diverse is in their mode of engagement with their world, or their way of “interfacing”. I personally know women who grasp Hegel (the logico-intellectual composer of this age par excellence) more intuitively and intellectually than many of my peers in groups dedicated to understanding Hegel, however, only one actively sustains the research that German Idealism demands. This is less about capacity and more about orientation. Our current “intellectual” climate is not biased toward men per se, but towards a more “masculine” mode of interfacing; through abstraction, systematizing, compartmentalization, etc. When that mode is treated as neutral, half of human experience is filtered out. I suppose it must also be said that marginalization should not be accepted, if a member of the female sex/gender wants to be a girlboss CEO, her merit should be proven through the fruits she bears - although there are some important qualifications to make in the “feminism” section of the article below.

The Story

To move beyond our current impasse, we can turn to the framework of spiritual science found in anthroposophy. Such an approach does not contradict empirical observation, but does seek to understand into the realm which gives meaning and context to what we observe in the sensible world. It helps provide exactly what is missing via a coherent teleological narrative of human development in which sexual polarity plays a part.

Firstly, we will begin with “human”. To speak of “man” is to invoke more than a biological species; it is a designation of a universal category of being defined by the potential for self-conscious spirit and moral freedom in the context of a partnership with the cosmos. This potential is our shared telos, the inherent destination inscribed in our nature. The human being is thus a process of becoming, and describes the latent capacity to realize being-hood. This journey of course in not uniform. The very structure of a developmental process, where a universal potential seeks realization under conditions of freedom and through the resistance of matter, necessitates a spectrum of manifestation. Within the human family, this appears as a divergence in the pace and degree of awakening. Some individuals, through intensified activity and under certain cosmic and karmic conditions, crystallize the human telos earlier and more fully. They become the forerunners, those in whom the future form of humanity becomes partially visible today. They act as translators for higher possibilities into forms that can gradually be comprehended and integrated by the broader collective. On the other hand, the same freedom that allows for accelerated development implies the possibility of arrest. Others may remain more firmly embedded in earlier, more instinctual stages of the human archetype. They are the echoes of humanity’s past forms, persevering strengths and memories that the advancing vanguard party might otherwise lose in its forward striving. What is being described here is an intrinsic feature of a cosmos in evolution, and the feature of any class of being which undergoes true evolution (involving telos). There are those that are advanced, those that are on the right track, and those that lag behind. Between these three points exists a gradient, in the same way that a single seed produces a field of plants at different stages of bloom. The one human telos manifests across a field of consciousness in varying stages of realization.

Since we have established our telos as the universal potential for spirit to become self-aware and free, it is appropriate to discuss the core of this being as the “I”, or the ego. The ego is the genderless seat of consciousness. Its telos is to awaken to its own spiritual nature and participate in the evolution of the world. This potential cannot be realized abstractly and must be realized in the world, and this requires a journey of individuation, and a descent into a kind of “limitation” which forces it to develop self-awareness and moral capacity. This is where the differentiation into masculine and feminine principles becomes essential. The creation of two distinct, but complementary, modes of embodied existence is the next topic.

The cosmology of anthroposophy is derived from Rudolf Steiner’s clairvoyant (which has a stricter definition than colloquially understood) investigation of the “Akashic Record”. If in the middle of reading this “story” stemming from anthroposophy, it starts to become unbelievable, you can skip to a section where I extrapolate the logical and structural takeaways as it relates to gender and sex, or, alternatively, treat this all as metaphor. This research into the “Akashic Record” describes, among many things, human evolution across vast epochs and stretches of time. In this view, the current physical form of humanity is the result of a long process of densification and specialization. He posits that in a remote past named the Lemurian epoch (which I argue should be called Thermian), presented a world alien to what we know now, characterized by a state of fluidity and malleability that permeated the Earth and its life forms. The Earth was described as a semi-fluid, thick substance, compared to a “fairly thick muddy sauce or soup”, or a “thickish liquid”. The atmosphere was much denser than it is today, water was also “much more fluid”. Physical malleability was key, seeing as the human body and other organisms consisted of “soft plastic matter” because the Earth’s crust was not yet hardened. Forms could be shaped and changed more easily by will and spiritual forces. Plant and animal life existed in archaic, often gigantic forms. What are now small ferns were then mighty trees, forming vast forests. The animal kingdom was dominated by creatures of an “amphibious” character. The most prominent descriptions provided are of giant, slime-like organisms. One source describes “giant slime oysters” or jelly-like creatures so vast that “all of France could have easily fit” on their backs (talk about escargot). Since we are focused on man for the time being, we shall turn our attention there and inward. I only wished to portray a kind of equilibrium between being and the environment that being resides in, or in logical form a kind of commensurability with global and local, revealing their unity.

The human being existed in an androgynous/bisexual state. The physical body was soft and plastic, malleable towards the self-determined forces of man and the environment (this, according to Steiner, helped in the formation of organs, which is important for later). Many world religions and mythologies preserve the memory of humanity’s original bisexual state. For example, the oldest Ancient Greek gods were described as having two sexes; in the mystery temples, only hermaphroditic figures were present. The Zoroastrian Myth portrays the memory even clearer through its narratives. So what of reproduction? Well, reproduction was a process of self-fructification, as opposed to the dualistic act we are familiar with. Self-fructification is an influential concept to understand, because prior to the sexed distinction, modes of reproduction up until the middle of the “Lemurian” period evolved through several distinct non-sexual stages, culminating in the birth of biological sexuality as we know it. The modes progressed from the immediacy of self-generation, or sprouting, where the “astral” human” incorporated etheric matter into its form, grew, and then split, similar to a bud sprouting. It then continued as humans were still unified with what would later become bird and lower mammal species. The early Lemurian period still saw a “spiritualistic” reproduction, where reproduction occurred through the forces of Kama (astral desire/feeling), which was still dissolving in the general environment, and not yet individualized. In spiritual science, the figures of Adam Kadmon, from Hebrew esotericism, and the Giant Ymir, from North mythology, are preserved clairvoyant memories of a literal, prior state of being. These figures represent a lost primal unity between humanity, the Earth, and all life. Both myths depict a time when the Earth itself was a giant human form (and remember, this is not “man” as it is currently, human is a designation). Accordingly, the killing of Ymir, from whose body the world is fashioned (his flesh becoming Earth, bones becoming mountains, etc.) symbolizes the cosmic process of densification and differentiation. It is the death of this original unity. As the Earth’s matter condensed and cooled, the one unified being had to separate to allow for further evolution. This is a literal fact, according to spiritual science. To the casual reader, I can only appeal to its structural coherence, that the fact that in its earliest stages the entire etheric substance of the Earth consisted of undifferentiated human archetypes makes perfect logical sense.

Let us return again to the form of man in Lemuria, particularly on the nature of the soul of man. The human soul was inherently male-female (androgynous). In this early state, the soul could completely express its complete, bisexual nature directly onto the soft physical matter of the body. This is how sexual expression was related to man in those times. Regarding sexual reproduction, because each being contained both principles, reproduction was an internal process of self-fructification, as previously mentioned. A human being could bring forth another from within itself, without the need of a partner. The form of consciousness in man had humanity living in a dream-like, or picture-consciousness. People perceived the world primarily through hearing and touch, experiencing it as a flow of images and natural forces rather than clear, conceptual thoughts. They lacked memory, and a capacity for what we now call thinking.

The primary catalyst for the origin of the sexed distinction, this process of division, was the increasing density and solidification of the Earth’s substance. As matter became harder and more rigid, the soul could no longer perfectly impose its own bisexual (male-female) image upon it. Likewise, the physical form was forced by external nature to specialize. It could no longer sustain the full expression of both soul principles (Will and Imagination) in one form. Thus, it split into two specialized vessels. The male aspect of the soul was associated with Will (Iccha Shakti), and the female aspect with Imagination (Kriya Shakti or the rudimentary beginning of thought, with thought being described here as an inner generative process). With this new form, a loss of self-fructification (the force that was once directed entirely outward for reproduction) occurred, and was partially blocked. This surplus energy was redirected inward. When one considers that self-fructification can be read as reproduction, and follow that reproduction does not only mean sexual reproduction, but the upkeep and maintenance of being, the implication for this formative energy as that which builds and sustains the physical organism and drives reproduction becomes clearer. Previously, this force was entirely engaged in the act of self-generation and sustaining the plastic form of older man. As matter condensed and the body specialized into male or female form, this “one way street” became a dead end, so to say. An individual could no longer complete the creative circuit alone. A portion of this formative energy now existed as surplus to the external requirement of solitary reproduction, and was dammed up within the organism. Seeing as it was “dammed up”, this energy looked for a new outlet and so it turned inward and upward, becoming the building substance for a new interiority (the refined organization necessary for thought, memory, and individuated consciousness as ego). Steiner describes this as the fructification of the human being by the Spirit.

The middle of the Lemurian epoch brought about the division of sexes from hermaphroditic, or androgynous man, for this reason. Another connection one may bring up is that the transition from the dreamy, picture-like consciousness (where one lived within spiritual images and directly perceived natural forces) to a sensory, brain-bound consciousness is the structure of the “Fall” of man, of his necessary descent into matter. The soul, to gain self-awareness had to build a physical body, with specialized sense organs, redirect creative energy inward to form a brain, and begin to rely on the senses and, eventually, logical thought to construct the “picture” of the world. There are a few notable consequences to this “Fall”. The first is the loss of intuitive knowledge. Humanity, as a whole, lost the old, instinctive clairvoyance and direct perception of spiritual beings and natural forces. The rudimentary emergence of the ego as a result of the withholding of the formative energy/soul-force inward (due, again, to the densification of the world and of man) resulted in the creation of an interior world for man. This separation from the outer world led to self-interest, or selfishness (and by extension desire). Likewise, man’s striving for inward development became selfish. This selfishness will play an interesting part in our story soon enough. On the other hand, there is a positive side. With isolated selfhood and sensory-based inner activity came the capacity for free choice, personal judgment, and moral responsibility. This is otherwise known as the true origin of Good and Evil. Man was no longer purely instinctive or guided by direct spiritual impulse.

To recap for the sake of brevity, a division of man from bi-sexuality to uni-sexuality occurred. The two sexes developed along different, but complementary paths, particularly in early Lemurian society. The “Masculine Path” (Will) focused on mastery over external natural forces and the physical world. Education for boys involved extreme hardening and endurance to strengthen the will and kill the carnal, freeing the will-power (Iccha Shakti). Men were seen as conduits for divine, natural powers. The “Feminine Path” (Imagination/Memory) focused on absorbing inner meaning and the spiritual qualities of nature. Education for girls cultivated a dreamy imagination, and a calm receptivity in the face of natural beauty and terror, such that they should serve as poised conveyors of what nature says. Within temples, initiates engaged in direct “intercourse with the Beings who work at the building of the world itself”. This gazed into the “creative laboratory of Nature”, perceiving and participating in the spiritual forces behind the world. This experience consecrated the initiates (who, again, were female), and their very presence exerted a natural branded magical influence on others. It is not explicitly stated that this activity was segregated by sex, but it does establish that women developed the essential faculties that would qualify them for such work. Through this, women developed the first germs of memory, and the earliest moral concepts of “good and evil”. They became the first interpreters of nature and the originators of a more personal, inner life.

The Âkâshic Records reveal what are in this respect scenes of much beauty. One of these shall be described. We are in a wood close to a gigantic tree. The sun has just risen in the east. Mighty are the shadows thrown by the palm-like tree across the cleared space round it. With her face to the east, and in a state of exaltation, we discern a priestess on a seat prepared of curious natural objects and plants. Slowly and in a rhythmic cadence flow from her lips certain wondrous sounds which are repeated again and again. Ranged in large circles, a number of men and women sit round her with dreamy faces, absorbing inner life from the sounds. Still other scenes may be witnessed. At another place, arranged in like manner, a priestess chants in a similar way, but her tones have in them something more mighty, more powerful, and the men around her move in rhythmic dances. For this was the other method by means of which the “soul” entered mankind. The mysterious rhythms which man had caught from Nature were imitated in the movements of his own limbs. Thus was it that man felt himself at one with Nature and with the Powers that ruled her.
—Steiner, GA 11

The transition to single-sexedness was guided by advanced spiritual entities. As mentioned previously, in every age, there are those who are “more” advanced, those that lag behind, and those in the Goldilocks zone of development. Likewise, there were superhuman beings who previously inhabited the hermaphroditic forms. These beings are termed the “Arhats”, Sons of the Fire Nebula. They are the most advanced Pitris (Lunar Fathers). Their consciousness evolved on a previous state of Earth’s evolution, completing their “human” stage there, becoming a kind of angelic humanity. In Lemuria, they acted as custodians of the pre-sexed humanity. After the sex division, they could no longer fully incarnate, and refused to incarnate, seeing as they require a balanced vessel. For if they incarnated into a one-sided sexed body, it would have meant a descent into a lower, “unfree” state for them (although, there are exceptions called the “Great Hermaphrodites”: the highest Arhats who did momentarily incarnate at the cusp of the transition to guide the process directly. They possessed the full, balanced power of the ancient hermaphroditic form, and were seen as divine messengers). Because of this, they worked from the spiritual world to implant sexual love and the impulse for reproduction as a sacrificial service in order that it continue humanity. They regulated sexual life. A less advanced group of Lunar Pitris, or beings who had undergone a different evolutionary path, were also presented. They acted as the bridge between the spiritual guides and the developing humanity. Unlike the Arhats who were not receptive to sensory impressions, these beings were receptive to both spiritual impressions and sensory impressions. Their task was to attract human consciousness toward the external, sensory world, enabling the development of sense-based perception and free will.

Brief note on the Moon: the recap above would be incomplete if it did not account for Lucifer, the Luciferic Spirits, and the moon. I will only briefly touch on these, seeing as we do not want to get too bogged down in the details. Previously, it was said that the Earth “hardened”. The stimulus for this was the departure of the Moon from the Earth, leaving a scar now characterized by the Ring of Fire. Before the Moon separated in the middle of the Lemurian Epoch, the description of Earth as provided held. It was the Moon’s exit that caused the Earth to harden to its current mineralistic state. This separation initiated the Earth’s rotation, creating the rhythms of day and night and the seasonal year. It can be said that the departure of the moon established the primary duality and polarity in the natural world into which the now dual-human form (male and female) would incarnate. The Earth became “cradled in between Sun and Moon influences”. The departing moon took with it the “terrible instincts” and “all the evil influences” that had developed in humanity’s astral body during a prior hellish phase. This purification allowed a fresh start for beings capable of higher evolution on the now-hardened Earth. The aforementioned “terrible instincts” and “evil influences” that had developed in man did not cease to be. They became associated with the moon as a separate cosmic body. From this new position, their influence could on now work upon the Earth and humanity from the outside, rather than being mixed indistinguishably from within. Soon after the Moon’s departure, the Elohim, or Jehovah beings, performed a great sacrifice to advance human evolution. Their act was to infuse the germ of the human “I”, or ego, into the human astral body. This was a slow, gradual process of “rippling in” the I-consciousness, creating the previously talked about new interiority. The “backwards spirits” from the Old Moon (the Luciferic beings) saw in this process a chance: having not completed their own evolution on the old moon, they found a new field for their development. So, they intercepted the process. As the Elohim poured the I-substance into the human astral body, the Luciferic beings rushed into that same astral body from the “other side”, attempting to merge with and take possession of this newly forming center of human consciousness. They blended their own attributes of brilliant intellect, fiery passion for independence, and a love of “freedom”, directly into the seat of human desires and emotions. “How can this be a bad thing?”, the Luciferic among you might ask. Well, it is a double-edged sword. The Elohim, or Jehovahic influence gave us lawful form and the foundational “I”, the capacity for reverence, and so on and so forth. But it also risks a humanity that would have developed in a state of innocence, with a childlike obedience, lacking true individuality and the capacity for self-chosen good. Likewise, the Luciferic influence (Backward Moon Spirits) gave us self-consciousness, artistic inspiration, intellectual brilliance, and the drive for independence. Their (or his, both are valid) “infection” brought along with it passionate desire, pride, rebellion, the ability to lie (there is no use lying if the mode of communication used in the past was a kind of telepathy, and there was no compulsion to lie since an interiority in the past wasn’t developed), and the temptation to use freedom selfishly against the divine laws. This union of the two influences, along with the departure of the moon, and descent of the world and man into matter, is the Biblical “Fall”. We have before us an astrological schema, wherein the Moon’s physical separation created the setup for the battle over the soul of man. The Sun remained the center of the Christ impulse, the Moon became the externalized base for the Luciferic influence, and the Earth became the arena where the human “I” caught between these influences, must exercise its freedom to choose its path. This “infection” still affects us today, manifest in specific traits. However, enough have been said on this topic.

If the Lemurian epoch (at least, starting in the middle of it) is characterized by a more “masculine” mode of interface- that of powerful will and mastery over the body and natural forces, then over time this mode would start to develop a more “feminine” bias towards highly developed memory. Therefore, this change, this metamorphosis of consciousness, physicality, and civilization marks a definitive step away from an instinctual union with nature, towards a more self-aware historically grounded existence. This new epoch is termed the “Atlantean”. Lemurian consciousness was a dreamlike picture-consciousness, where powerful will and direct perception of nature reigned, but it also lacked memory. No memory existed at this time to retain ideas/concepts or experiences. For this reason, the transitory state from the middle-Lemurian period, into the Atlantean found women with a specialized role. The education of girls was sharply differentiated from the “hardening” of boys, since it aimed at developing a specific soul quality. Girls were deliberately exposed to powerful natural phenomena and brutal fights between men. The intention wasn’t to provoke fear or aggression, but to allow them to perceive these events with calmness and feel an admiration for the display of strength and prowess, in an effort to foster a disposition to dreaming, to “revelling in fancy”. So, through their senses, women drew in the forces of nature, and allowed them to react upon their souls. This process formed “the germs of memory” as Steiner calls it, which to be clear was a monumental step in the development of human consciousness. True memory did not exist in humanity before this. Thus, the Atlantean awoke into a world of memory. While they did not yet possess our logical and abstract reason, they thought in vivid, remembered images and sense impressions. This newfound ability to recall the past gave their lives a sense of personal history and continuity, which became the very foundation of their societies. Perhaps this is what Plato meant when he said that all learning is remembering? He was, after all, an initiate of the Pythagorean mystery school. Although one can argue it was Socrates who said this, you can never tell if it’s Socrates speaking or Plato speaking through Socrates at first glance!.

Let us clairvoyantly observe the old continent of Atlantis, which must be sought where the Atlantic Ocean now lies, between Africa and Europe on the one side, and America on the other. This continent was encircled by a sort of warm stream, a stream about which clairvoyant consciousness reveals that, strange as it may sound, it flowed upwards from the South, through Baffins Bay, towards the north of Greenland, encircling it and then, flowing over to the East, gradually cooled down; then, at a time when Siberia and Russia had not yet risen to the surface, it flowed down near the Ural mountains, turned, touched the Eastern Carpathians, flowed into the region occupied by the present Sahara, and finally streamed towards the Atlantic Ocean near the Bay of Biscay; so that it flowed in a perfectly unbroken stream. You will understand that only the remnants of this stream still remain. This is the Gulf Stream, which at that time encircled the Atlantean Continent. You will now also understand that, with the Greeks, the life of the soul is remembrance. The picture of Oceanos arose in them, which is a memory of that Atlantean epoch. Their picture of the world is not so very incorrect, because it was drawn from the old Atlantean epoch. The stream that came down by Spitzbergen as a warm current, and gradually cooled and so on, — the region encircled by this stream the Chinese have literally reproduced by enclosing within their Great Wall the culture which they rescued from the Atlantean epoch. There was as yet no history in the Atlantean civilization, hence the Chinese civilization is also in some ways lacking in history. Thus we have there something pre-Indian, something coming from Atlantis.
—Steiner, GA 121

Trust and authority was suited towards those with the longest experience, for they had the most “knowledge” to draw upon. Hence, education was focused on imprinting life’s images upon the student for future recall. This cognitive evolution was mirrored by a change in man’s physical environment and technological mastery, a topic we will not discuss in detail here. By physical environment, it is important to emphasize a change in the world. The Moon’s separation triggered the final hardening of Earth and it’s atmosphere. Earth, now hardened during the Atlantean epoch, had a much denser atmosphere, much “thinner” water, enabling Atlantean technology (e.g., vehicles that floated above the ground). Where the Lemurian was a “born magician” (as Steiner says) who shaped soft matter through will, the Atlantean became a bio-technician. They learned to harness the dormant life force within plant seeds, transforming this energy into technical power for vehicles that floated above the ground, representing a civilization built on a learned (albeit still image-based) manipulation of nature’s subtler forces. The fabric of human interaction was rewoven. The Lemurian wordless “thought-reading” gave way to the development of true language in Atlantis. For the first time, experiences could be communicated, names given to things, and a social substance was created through speech. Social structures also transformed.
